stem: The speed of sound in soft tissue is approximately:
rationale: The average speed of sound in soft tissue is 1540 m/s (1.54 mm/μs). This value is assumed by ultrasound machines for all distance calculations.

stem: Increasing the transducer frequency will:
options: Improve axial resolution but decrease penetration depth
Increase penetration depth and decrease resolution
Have no effect on image quality
rationale: Higher frequency = shorter wavelength = better axial resolution but increased attenuation = less penetration. This is the fundamental frequency-resolution-penetration tradeoff.

stem: Axial resolution is determined by:
options: Spatial pulse length (number of cycles × wavelength)
rationale: Axial resolution = SPL/2. Shorter spatial pulse length (fewer cycles or higher frequency) improves axial resolution. It cannot be adjusted by the sonographer after transducer selection.

stem: The piezoelectric effect in ultrasound transducers refers to:
options: The ability of certain crystals to convert electrical energy to mechanical energy and vice versa
The absorption of sound by tissue
The reflection of sound at interfaces
rationale: Piezoelectric crystals (PZT) vibrate when voltage is applied (transmit) and generate voltage when deformed by returning echoes (receive). This bidirectional conversion is the piezoelectric effect.

stem: The Doppler shift frequency is directly proportional to:
options: Blood flow velocity and transducer frequency, and inversely proportional to the speed of sound
Only the depth of the vessel
rationale: Doppler equation: fd = 2fo × v × cosθ / c. The shift is proportional to transmitted frequency (fo) and flow velocity (v), and inversely proportional to the speed of sound (c).

stem: Acoustic impedance is determined by:
options: The density of the medium multiplied by the speed of sound in that medium
Only the frequency of the transducer
rationale: Acoustic impedance (Z) = density (ρ) × speed of sound (c). Reflection occurs at interfaces where impedance differs. Large impedance mismatches = strong reflections.

stem: Which artifact results from the ultrasound beam encountering a strongly reflective interface?
rationale: Reverberation occurs when the sound bounces between two strong parallel reflectors (or between the transducer and a strong reflector), creating equally spaced false echoes.

stem: Acoustic shadowing is seen posterior to:
options: Highly attenuating structures like calculi and bone
rationale: Acoustic shadowing appears posterior to structures that strongly reflect or absorb sound (gallstones, renal calculi, bone), preventing sound transmission beyond them.

stem: Posterior acoustic enhancement is associated with:
options: Fluid-filled structures such as simple cysts
rationale: Fluid transmits sound with minimal attenuation, so structures posterior to fluid-filled cysts appear brighter (enhanced) compared to adjacent tissue at the same depth.

stem: The Nyquist limit in pulsed Doppler is:
options: The maximum velocity that can be measured
rationale: The Nyquist limit = PRF/2. Velocities exceeding this limit cause aliasing. To avoid aliasing: increase PRF, decrease frequency, increase angle, or use CW Doppler.

stem: Aliasing in color Doppler appears as:
options: Wrap-around of colors (red appears where blue should be and vice versa)
rationale: Aliasing occurs when flow velocity exceeds the Nyquist limit. In color Doppler, it appears as paradoxical color reversal (wrap-around) at the point of highest velocity.

stem: The near field (Fresnel zone) of an ultrasound beam:
options: Has a converging beam pattern from the transducer face to the focal point
Extends from the focal point to infinity
rationale: The near field extends from the transducer face to the focal point (natural focus). The beam converges, reaching its narrowest width at the focus. The far field diverges beyond the focus.

stem: Time Gain Compensation (TGC) is used to:
options: Compensate for attenuation by increasing gain for deeper structures
rationale: TGC applies progressively more amplification to echoes returning from deeper structures, compensating for the increased attenuation that occurs with depth.

stem: Lateral resolution is determined by:
options: Beam width at a given depth
rationale: Lateral resolution equals the beam width. It is best at the focal zone where the beam is narrowest. Unlike axial resolution, it varies with depth.

stem: Power Doppler differs from color Doppler by:
options: Displaying the amplitude/strength of the Doppler signal rather than velocity and direction
rationale: Power Doppler maps the integrated power (amplitude) of the Doppler signal. It is more sensitive to slow flow but does not display velocity or direction information.

stem: The duty factor in pulsed ultrasound represents:
options: The fraction of time the transducer is transmitting sound
The percentage of time receiving echoes
rationale: Duty factor = pulse duration / pulse repetition period × 100%. In imaging, the transducer transmits <1% of the time and listens >99%. CW Doppler has a 50% duty factor.

stem: Harmonic imaging reduces:
options: Near-field artifacts and improves contrast resolution by using the second harmonic frequency
rationale: Tissue harmonic imaging uses the 2nd harmonic frequency generated within tissue. Since harmonics are generated beyond the body wall, near-field artifacts and side lobe artifacts are reduced.

stem: The mechanical index (MI) relates to:
rationale: MI = peak rarefactional pressure / √frequency. It indicates the likelihood of cavitation — the formation and collapse of gas bubbles in tissue. FDA limit is MI ≤ 1.9.

stem: The thermal index (TI) indicates:
options: The potential temperature rise in tissue due to ultrasound exposure
The body temperature of the patient
rationale: TI estimates the potential temperature increase in tissue from ultrasound exposure. TI = transmitted power / power needed to raise tissue temperature by 1°C. Variants: TIS, TIB, TIC.

stem: ALARA principle in ultrasound means:
options: As Low As Reasonably Achievable — minimize exposure while obtaining diagnostic information
rationale: ALARA applies to ultrasound by using the minimum output power, shortest exposure time, and lowest mechanical/thermal indices necessary for diagnostic quality.

stem: Elevation resolution (slice thickness) is determined by:
options: The height of the transducer element or electronic focusing in the elevation plane
Beam width in the scan plane
rationale: Elevation resolution (perpendicular to the scan plane) is determined by element height and any elevation focusing. It causes slice thickness artifacts from partial volume averaging.

stem: Speckle in ultrasound images is caused by:
options: Constructive and destructive interference of scattered echoes from small structures
rationale: Speckle is a granular pattern resulting from interference of echoes scattered by structures smaller than the wavelength. Spatial compounding and speckle reduction algorithms can reduce it.

stem: A phased array transducer is characterized by:
options: Small footprint with electronic beam steering for cardiac and intercostal scanning
Large linear footprint for vascular scanning
rationale: Phased arrays have a small footprint with tightly spaced elements that electronically steer and focus the beam. Ideal for cardiac scanning through narrow intercostal windows.

stem: In spectral Doppler, a narrow spectral window (clear area under the waveform) indicates:
rationale: Laminar flow has uniform velocities creating a narrow spectral envelope with a clear spectral window. Turbulent flow fills in the window (spectral broadening) due to wide velocity variation.

stem: What is the purpose of a matching layer on a transducer?
options: Reducing the acoustic impedance mismatch between the transducer element and skin for efficient sound transmission
rationale: The matching layer has intermediate acoustic impedance between PZT crystal (~33 MRayls) and skin (~1.6 MRayls), reducing reflection at the interface for better sound transmission.

stem: The normal echogenicity of the liver parenchyma compared to the right kidney cortex is:
options: Slightly more echogenic (brighter) than the renal cortex
Less echogenic (darker) than the renal cortex
rationale: Normal liver is slightly more echogenic than the renal cortex. When the liver is markedly more echogenic than the kidney, it suggests hepatic steatosis (fatty liver).

stem: A simple hepatic cyst on ultrasound demonstrates:
options: Anechoic content, smooth thin walls, posterior acoustic enhancement, and no internal vascularity
Thick irregular walls with internal echoes
rationale: Simple cysts are anechoic, smooth-walled, round/oval with posterior enhancement and no Doppler flow. These benign findings require no follow-up.

stem: Sonographic Murphy's sign refers to:
options: Maximum tenderness elicited when the transducer is pressed over the gallbladder
rationale: Sonographic Murphy's sign = focal tenderness when the transducer compresses the gallbladder. It is highly specific for acute cholecystitis, especially when combined with gallstones.

stem: The Wall-Echo-Shadow (WES) sign is associated with:
rationale: WES sign occurs when the GB is packed with stones: anterior wall (W), echogenic stones (E), and posterior shadowing (S). The GB lumen is not visualized.

stem: The normal common bile duct diameter in an adult should be less than:
options: 6-7 mm (up to 10 mm in elderly or post-cholecystectomy)
rationale: Normal CBD measures <6-7 mm. Add 1 mm per decade over age 60. Post-cholecystectomy CBD may measure up to 10 mm without obstruction.

stem: Hydronephrosis on ultrasound is characterized by:
options: Dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces with anechoic fluid-filled spaces
rationale: Hydronephrosis shows dilated renal pelvis and calyces as interconnecting anechoic spaces within the renal sinus. Severity graded from mild (pelvis only) to severe (cortical thinning).

stem: A renal calculus on ultrasound typically demonstrates:
options: An echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing
rationale: Renal stones appear as echogenic foci with posterior acoustic shadowing. Twinkle artifact on color Doppler (rapidly alternating color behind the stone) can also aid detection.

stem: The normal sonographic appearance of the pancreas is:
options: Echogenicity equal to or slightly greater than the liver, with a smooth contour
rationale: Normal pancreas is isoechoic to slightly hyperechoic relative to the liver. It becomes more echogenic with age due to fatty infiltration.

stem: Acute pancreatitis on ultrasound may show:
options: Diffuse enlargement of the pancreas with decreased echogenicity and possible peripancreatic fluid
rationale: Acute pancreatitis shows enlarged, hypoechoic (edematous) pancreas with possible peripancreatic fluid collections. The pancreas may appear normal on US in mild cases.

stem: The sonographic appearance of a hepatic hemangioma is typically:
options: Well-defined hyperechoic mass with posterior acoustic enhancement
rationale: Cavernous hemangiomas are typically well-defined, hyperechoic, homogeneous masses <3 cm with possible posterior enhancement. They are the most common benign liver tumor.

stem: Fatty infiltration of the liver appears as:
options: Diffusely increased hepatic echogenicity with decreased visualization of hepatic vessels and diaphragm
rationale: Hepatic steatosis shows diffusely increased echogenicity (bright liver), poor penetration, decreased visualization of hepatic veins and diaphragm, and increased hepatorenal contrast.

stem: The normal spleen length should not exceed:
rationale: Normal spleen measures <12-13 cm in length. Splenomegaly is defined as >13 cm. Common causes include portal hypertension, infection, hematologic disorders.

stem: The target sign (bulls-eye pattern) in the bowel may indicate:
options: Intussusception or bowel wall thickening from inflammation or neoplasm
rationale: The target/bulls-eye sign shows concentric rings of echogenicity in cross-section, seen with intussusception (telescoping bowel), bowel wall edema, or neoplastic infiltration.

stem: Choledocholithiasis refers to:
options: Stones in the common bile duct
rationale: Choledocholithiasis = stones in the CBD. It may cause biliary obstruction with upstream ductal dilation. US has limited sensitivity (~50-80%) for CBD stones.

stem: A Riedel lobe is:
options: A normal anatomic variant — a tongue-like extension of the right hepatic lobe
rationale: Riedel lobe is a normal variant where the right lobe extends inferiorly below the costal margin. It can be mistaken for hepatomegaly.

stem: The main portal vein normally measures:
rationale: Normal main portal vein diameter is <13 mm. Dilation (>13 mm) suggests portal hypertension. Flow should be hepatopetal (toward the liver).

stem: Hepatic cirrhosis on ultrasound may show:
options: Nodular liver surface, coarsened echotexture, caudate lobe hypertrophy, ascites, and portal hypertension signs
Smooth liver surface with normal echotexture
rationale: Cirrhosis shows surface nodularity, heterogeneous/coarse echotexture, possible right lobe atrophy/caudate hypertrophy, portal hypertension signs (ascites, splenomegaly, varices).

stem: An aortic aneurysm is defined as aortic diameter greater than:
rationale: Abdominal aortic aneurysm is defined as diameter >3.0 cm (normal aorta <2.0 cm). Aneurysms >5.5 cm typically warrant surgical intervention due to rupture risk.

stem: The sonographic finding of parallel channel sign (double barrel shotgun sign) in the liver indicates:
options: Dilated intrahepatic bile ducts running parallel to portal vein branches
rationale: The parallel channel sign shows dilated intrahepatic bile ducts running alongside portal vein branches, creating a 'double barrel shotgun' appearance indicating biliary obstruction.

stem: A junctional fold in the gallbladder:
options: Is a normal anatomic variant that may mimic a septum or mass
rationale: A junctional fold is a normal fold in the gallbladder wall, often at the junction of the body and infundibulum. It can mimic a septum or mass but is a benign variant.

stem: The gestational sac is first visible on transvaginal ultrasound at approximately:
rationale: The gestational sac is first seen on TVUS at ~4.5-5 weeks when β-hCG reaches ~1000-2000 mIU/mL. Transabdominal detection is typically 1-2 weeks later.

stem: Crown-rump length (CRL) is the most accurate measurement for dating in the:
rationale: CRL is the most accurate biometric measurement for gestational age dating in the first trimester, with an accuracy of ±3-5 days up to 13+6 weeks.

stem: Nuchal translucency (NT) measurement is performed between:
rationale: NT screening is performed at 11+0 to 13+6 weeks (CRL 45-84 mm). Increased NT (>3 mm) is associated with chromosomal abnormalities, cardiac defects, and other anomalies.

stem: The four standard measurements for fetal biometry in the 2nd/3rd trimester are:
rationale: Standard biometric measurements: Biparietal Diameter (BPD), Head Circumference (HC), Abdominal Circumference (AC), and Femur Length (FL). These estimate gestational age and fetal weight.

stem: The most accurate single measurement for estimating fetal weight in the third trimester is:
rationale: AC correlates best with fetal weight because it reflects both fetal fat and liver size. Multi-parameter formulas (Hadlock) combining BPD, HC, AC, FL provide the most accurate EFW.

stem: Placenta previa is defined as:
options: Placenta covering or within 2 cm of the internal cervical os
Placenta that has separated from the uterine wall
rationale: Placenta previa = placental tissue covering/adjacent to the internal os. Low-lying = within 2 cm. Transvaginal US is the gold standard for diagnosis. Most resolve by third trimester.

stem: An ectopic pregnancy is most commonly located in:
options: The fallopian tube (ampulla being the most common tubal location)
rationale: ~95% of ectopic pregnancies are tubal, with the ampulla being the most common location. US findings: empty uterus, adnexal mass, free fluid. Direct visualization of tubal ectopic is ideal.

stem: The yolk sac is typically visible when the mean sac diameter reaches:
rationale: The yolk sac should be visible when MSD reaches 8-10 mm. Absence of a yolk sac in a sac >8 mm on TVUS raises concern for anembryonic pregnancy.

stem: Oligohydramnios refers to:
options: Decreased amniotic fluid volume (AFI <5 cm or deepest pocket <2 cm)
rationale: Oligohydramnios = AFI <5 cm or maximum vertical pocket <2 cm. Causes include PPROM, renal agenesis, IUGR, and post-term pregnancy.

stem: Polyhydramnios is defined as:
options: Excess amniotic fluid (AFI >24 cm or deepest pocket >8 cm)
Normal amniotic fluid in twin pregnancy
rationale: Polyhydramnios = AFI >24 cm or MVP >8 cm. Causes include GDM, fetal anomalies (GI obstruction, neural tube defects), twin-to-twin transfusion, and idiopathic.

stem: A complete molar pregnancy on ultrasound characteristically shows:
options: Snowstorm appearance with multiple small cystic spaces filling the uterus without a fetus
A normal fetus with large placenta
A small gestational sac with heartbeat
rationale: Complete molar pregnancy shows a 'snowstorm' or 'cluster of grapes' pattern: multiple small vesicles (hydropic villi) filling the uterus without an identifiable embryo. β-hCG is markedly elevated.

stem: The biophysical profile (BPP) evaluates:
options: Fetal breathing, movement, tone, amniotic fluid volume, and non-stress test
rationale: BPP scores 5 components (2 points each, max 10): fetal breathing movements, gross body movements, fetal tone, amniotic fluid volume, and reactive NST.

stem: Intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) is suggested by:
options: Estimated fetal weight below the 10th percentile for gestational age with abnormal Doppler findings
Normal amniotic fluid with normal growth
rationale: IUGR is EFW <10th percentile. Additional concerning findings include abnormal umbilical artery Doppler (absent/reversed end-diastolic flow), oligohydramnios, and asymmetric growth.

stem: Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) occurs in:
rationale: TTTS occurs in monochorionic (shared placenta) twins due to unbalanced arteriovenous anastomoses. The donor twin has oligohydramnios; the recipient has polyhydramnios.

stem: The normal fetal heart rate range is:
rationale: Normal FHR: 110-160 bpm in 2nd/3rd trimesters. Early first trimester FHR may be lower (100-120 bpm at 6-7 weeks). Bradycardia (<110) or tachycardia (>160) requires evaluation.

stem: Normal internal carotid artery (ICA) peak systolic velocity is:
rationale: Normal ICA PSV is <125 cm/s. PSV 125-230 cm/s suggests 50-69% stenosis. PSV >230 cm/s suggests ≥70% stenosis per Society of Radiologists in Ultrasound criteria.

stem: An ICA/CCA PSV ratio >4.0 suggests:
rationale: ICA/CCA ratio >4.0 correlates with ≥70% stenosis. Ratio 2.0-4.0 suggests 50-69% stenosis. The ratio helps when absolute velocities may be unreliable.

stem: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is diagnosed by:
options: Non-compressibility of the vein with direct visualization of intraluminal thrombus
rationale: The primary criterion for DVT is non-compressibility of the vein. Additional findings: visible thrombus, absence of color fill, lack of augmentation, and absent spontaneous flow.

stem: Augmentation in venous Doppler refers to:
options: Increased venous flow velocity with distal compression or release of proximal compression
rationale: Augmentation tests venous patency: squeezing distal to the probe should increase flow (distal augmentation). Absence suggests obstruction between the compression site and probe.

stem: A monophasic arterial waveform suggests:
options: Significant proximal arterial disease or distal to a stenosis
rationale: Normal peripheral arterial waveforms are triphasic (forward flow, brief reversal, small forward). Loss of the reverse component (biphasic) then loss of triphasic pattern (monophasic) indicates proximal disease.

stem: The vertebral artery normally demonstrates:
options: Antegrade (toward the brain) low-resistance flow similar to the ICA
rationale: Normal vertebral artery flow is antegrade (toward the brain) with a low-resistance waveform similar to the ICA. Reversed flow suggests subclavian steal syndrome.

stem: Color flow Doppler of a normal patent vein shows:
options: Complete color fill of the lumen that augments with distal compression
rationale: A normal patent vein shows complete color filling of the lumen. Color should augment (increase) with distal compression. Absence or incomplete fill suggests thrombus.

stem: Subclavian steal syndrome on ultrasound shows:
options: Reversed (retrograde) flow in the ipsilateral vertebral artery
Normal antegrade flow in both vertebral arteries
Complete absence of all cervical arterial flow
rationale: Subclavian steal: proximal subclavian stenosis/occlusion causes reversed vertebral artery flow (retrograde) to supply the arm. The contralateral vertebral compensates.

stem: The normal ankle-brachial index (ABI) range is:
rationale: Normal ABI: 0.9-1.3. ABI <0.9 suggests PAD. ABI <0.5 indicates severe disease. ABI >1.3 may indicate non-compressible calcified arteries (common in diabetes).

stem: Phasicity in venous Doppler refers to:
options: Variation in venous flow with the respiratory cycle (decreases with inspiration in lower extremities)
rationale: Normal venous phasicity shows flow variation with respiration. In lower extremities, flow decreases with inspiration (increased abdominal pressure) and increases with expiration.

stem: A Baker's cyst is located:
options: In the popliteal fossa, posterior to the knee joint
rationale: A Baker's (popliteal) cyst is a fluid collection in the popliteal fossa communicating with the knee joint through the semimembranosus-gastrocnemius bursa.

stem: Tardus-parvus waveform in the renal artery suggests:
rationale: Tardus-parvus: delayed systolic upstroke (tardus) with diminished amplitude (parvus). Seen distal to a hemodynamically significant renal artery stenosis (>60%).

stem: Normal endometrial thickness in a postmenopausal woman not on hormone replacement should be:
rationale: Postmenopausal endometrium should measure <4-5 mm (single layer). Thickness >4-5 mm in a postmenopausal woman with bleeding warrants further evaluation (biopsy).

stem: The endometrial thickness in the proliferative phase of the menstrual cycle appears:
options: Thin, hypoechoic, and gradually thickening (4-8 mm)
rationale: During the proliferative (follicular) phase, estrogen causes endometrial growth from 4-8 mm. It appears hypoechoic/homogeneous with a thin echogenic line.

stem: A uterine fibroid (leiomyoma) on ultrasound typically appears as:
options: A well-defined hypoechoic solid mass that may cause uterine contour abnormality and shadow
An irregular mass with posterior enhancement
rationale: Fibroids are typically well-defined, hypoechoic (but variable), solid masses arising from the myometrium. They may show calcifications with shadowing and edge shadowing.

stem: The most common location for an ectopic pregnancy is:
options: The ampullary portion of the fallopian tube
The interstitial portion of the tube
rationale: ~70% of ectopic pregnancies occur in the ampulla. The isthmus accounts for ~12%, fimbrial ~11%, and interstitial ~2-3%.

stem: A dermoid cyst (mature teratoma) on ultrasound may show:
options: A complex mass with fat-fluid level, calcifications, hair, and Rokitansky nodule
rationale: Dermoids are the most common benign ovarian neoplasm. Findings include fat-fluid level, echogenic Rokitansky nodule (dermoid plug), calcification (teeth/bone), and the 'tip of the iceberg' sign.

stem: Endometriosis on ultrasound is most commonly visualized as:
options: An endometrioma — a cyst with homogeneous low-level internal echoes (chocolate cyst)
rationale: Endometriomas appear as well-defined cysts with homogeneous low-level echoes (ground glass appearance) due to old blood products. They are called 'chocolate cysts'.

stem: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) on ultrasound shows:
options: Enlarged ovaries with ≥12 peripheral follicles measuring 2-9 mm (string of pearls appearance)
rationale: PCOS criteria include ≥12 follicles (2-9 mm) per ovary and/or ovarian volume >10 mL. The 'string of pearls' pattern shows peripherally arranged small follicles.

stem: Normal ovarian volume in a reproductive-age woman is approximately:
rationale: Normal ovarian volume = L × W × H × 0.523, typically <10 mL in reproductive age. Volume >10 mL in PCOS context is significant.

stem: An intrauterine device (IUD) on ultrasound appears as:
options: A highly echogenic linear structure within the endometrial cavity
rationale: IUDs appear as echogenic linear structures within the uterine cavity, often with posterior shadowing/reverberation artifacts. String may be visible at the cervix.

stem: Adenomyosis on ultrasound may show:
options: Heterogeneous myometrium with myometrial cysts, asymmetric wall thickening, and indistinct endometrial-myometrial border
rationale: Adenomyosis shows heterogeneous myometrium with subendometrial cysts, asymmetric wall thickening (usually posterior), indistinct endo-myometrial junction, and linear myometrial striations.

stem: The resistive index (RI) of a normal ovarian artery is:
options: Variable with menstrual cycle — lower in the luteal phase (0.4-0.5) due to corpus luteum
rationale: Ovarian RI varies with cycle. Luteal phase shows lower RI (~0.4-0.5) due to increased vascularity of the corpus luteum. Follicular phase RI is typically higher.

stem: A thyroid nodule with microcalcifications on ultrasound is concerning because:
options: Microcalcifications are associated with papillary thyroid carcinoma
rationale: Microcalcifications (punctate bright echogenic foci without shadowing) are the most specific US feature of papillary thyroid cancer. Other suspicious features: hypoechoic, taller-than-wide, irregular margins.

stem: The ACR TI-RADS system classifies thyroid nodules by:
options: Assigning points based on composition, echogenicity, shape, margin, and echogenic foci to determine management
rationale: TI-RADS assigns points: composition, echogenicity, shape, margin, and echogenic foci. Total points determine category (TR1-5) and management (no FNA vs. FNA vs. follow-up).

stem: A parathyroid adenoma on ultrasound typically appears as:
options: A hypoechoic, hypervascular oval mass posterior to the thyroid gland
A hyperechoic mass anterior to the thyroid
An anechoic cyst within the thyroid
rationale: Parathyroid adenomas are typically hypoechoic, oval, hypervascular masses located posterior to the thyroid gland. They are the most common cause of primary hyperparathyroidism.

stem: Normal testicular echogenicity is:
rationale: Normal testes have homogeneous medium-level echogenicity. Any focal hypoechoic or heterogeneous area within the testis is suspicious for malignancy until proven otherwise.

stem: Testicular torsion on ultrasound shows:
options: Absent or decreased intratesticular blood flow on color Doppler with an enlarged, hypoechoic testis
Increased blood flow to the affected testis
rationale: Testicular torsion: absent/decreased intratesticular flow on the affected side. The testis may be enlarged and hypoechoic (edematous). Compare with the normal contralateral side.

stem: A breast cyst on ultrasound demonstrates:
options: Anechoic, well-circumscribed, round/oval mass with posterior enhancement and thin imperceptible wall
Irregular hypoechoic mass with posterior shadowing
rationale: Simple breast cysts are round/oval, anechoic, well-circumscribed with posterior enhancement and edge refraction shadows. They are BI-RADS 2 (benign).

stem: The BI-RADS ultrasound assessment category 4 indicates:
rationale: BI-RADS 4 = suspicious abnormality requiring tissue sampling. Subcategories: 4A (low suspicion, 2-10%), 4B (moderate, 10-50%), 4C (high, 50-95%).

stem: An epididymal cyst (spermatocele) appears as:
options: An anechoic or low-level echo cyst in the head of the epididymis
A solid mass in the testis
rationale: Spermatoceles are cystic structures arising from the epididymal head, typically anechoic or with low-level echoes (spermatozoa). They are benign and usually incidental.

stem: The normal thyroid gland on ultrasound is:
options: Homogeneous, medium to high echogenicity, with smooth borders
rationale: Normal thyroid is homogeneous with medium-high echogenicity (more echogenic than surrounding strap muscles), smooth borders, and visible isthmus connecting the lobes.

stem: Graves' disease on thyroid ultrasound may show:
options: Diffusely enlarged gland with decreased echogenicity and markedly increased vascularity (thyroid inferno)
rationale: Graves' disease shows diffuse thyroid enlargement, decreased echogenicity, and dramatically increased vascularity ('thyroid inferno' on color Doppler).

stem: A complete rotator cuff tear on ultrasound shows:
options: Full-thickness defect in the tendon with non-visualization of the tendon or hypoechoic/anechoic cleft
rationale: Full-thickness tears show complete disruption: non-visualization of tendon, hypoechoic/anechoic cleft through the full thickness, or retraction of the tendon stump.

stem: Normal tendons on ultrasound appear:
options: Fibrillar (parallel echogenic lines) and hyperechoic compared to muscle
rationale: Normal tendons show a characteristic fibrillar pattern (organized parallel echogenic lines) and are hyperechoic. Anisotropy can cause false hypoechogenicity if beam angle isn't perpendicular.

stem: Anisotropy in musculoskeletal ultrasound refers to:
options: Artifactual hypoechogenicity of a tendon when the beam is not perpendicular to the tendon fibers
rationale: Anisotropy is the most important pitfall in MSK US. Tendons appear falsely hypoechoic when the beam strikes non-perpendicularly. Heel-toe maneuver confirms artifact vs. pathology.

stem: A ganglion cyst on ultrasound appears as:
options: A well-defined anechoic or hypoechoic cystic mass, often adjacent to a joint or tendon sheath
rationale: Ganglion cysts are well-defined, usually anechoic (may have internal debris), lobulated cystic masses arising from joint capsules or tendon sheaths. Most common at the dorsal wrist.

stem: Tendinopathy on ultrasound may show:
options: Thickened, hypoechoic tendon with loss of the normal fibrillar pattern and possible neovascularity
rationale: Tendinopathy shows tendon thickening, decreased echogenicity, loss of fibrillar pattern, possible calcifications, and neovascularity on power Doppler.

stem: A Baker's cyst (popliteal cyst) communicates with:
options: The knee joint via the gastrocnemius-semimembranosus bursa
rationale: Baker's cysts are synovial fluid-filled herniations between the medial head of the gastrocnemius and semimembranosus tendons, communicating with the knee joint.

stem: Ultrasound-guided aspiration and injection techniques require:
options: Real-time visualization of the needle tip to ensure accurate placement
rationale: US-guided procedures use real-time imaging to visualize needle trajectory and tip position, ensuring accurate targeting of fluid collections, joints, or therapeutic injection sites.

stem: Carpal tunnel syndrome on ultrasound may show:
options: Enlarged, hypoechoic median nerve at the carpal tunnel inlet with cross-sectional area >10-12 mm²
rationale: CTS shows enlarged median nerve (CSA >10-12 mm² at the pisiform level), loss of fascicular pattern, increased echogenicity of flexor retinaculum, and possible bowing.

stem: The 'starry sky' liver pattern on ultrasound suggests:
options: Acute hepatitis with diffusely decreased liver echogenicity and prominent portal triads
rationale: The 'starry sky' pattern: diffusely hypoechoic liver (edema) with brightly echogenic portal triad walls standing out like stars. Seen in acute hepatitis and toxic shock.

stem: Budd-Chiari syndrome on ultrasound shows:
options: Hepatic vein thrombosis with absence of normal hepatic vein flow and possible caudate lobe hypertrophy
rationale: Budd-Chiari: hepatic vein thrombosis/obstruction. US shows absent/reversed hepatic vein flow, caudate lobe hypertrophy (independent drainage), hepatomegaly, and ascites.

stem: A 'ring of fire' on color Doppler of the ovary suggests:
options: Ectopic pregnancy (tubal ring) or corpus luteum with marked peripheral vascularity
rationale: Ring of fire = intense peripheral vascularity. In the ovary, it may represent a corpus luteum (normal) or tubal ring of an ectopic pregnancy. Clinical correlation is essential.

stem: Sandpaper gallbladder wall refers to:
options: Diffuse adenomyomatosis of the gallbladder wall with intramural diverticula (Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses)
rationale: Adenomyomatosis shows gallbladder wall thickening with intramural diverticula (RAS) creating comet-tail artifacts. The 'sandpaper' appearance reflects diffuse wall involvement.

stem: The 'pseudokidney' sign in the bowel suggests:
options: Bowel wall thickening, mass, or intussusception creating a kidney-like cross-sectional appearance
rationale: The pseudokidney sign: a bowel segment with thickened wall surrounding the central echogenic mucosa, mimicking a kidney on longitudinal view. Seen in intussusception and bowel tumors.

stem: Courvoisier's sign on ultrasound refers to:
options: A palpable/visible enlarged gallbladder with biliary obstruction, typically from pancreatic head malignancy
rationale: Courvoisier's sign: a distended, non-tender gallbladder with obstructive jaundice suggests malignant obstruction (pancreatic head mass) rather than stone disease.

stem: Mirizzi syndrome is characterized by:
options: External compression of the common hepatic duct by an impacted cystic duct or gallbladder neck stone
rationale: Mirizzi syndrome: a gallstone impacted in the cystic duct or GB neck causes extrinsic compression of the common hepatic duct, leading to obstructive jaundice.

stem: What determines the PRF in pulsed ultrasound?
rationale: PRF is determined by the maximum imaging depth. Deeper imaging requires longer round-trip time, reducing PRF. PRF = c / (2 × depth).

stem: Temporal resolution refers to:
options: The ability to accurately track moving structures over time, determined by frame rate
The ability to distinguish two structures side by side
The ability to resolve along the beam axis
The ability to detect echoes from deep structures
rationale: Temporal resolution = frame rate. Higher frame rate = better temporal resolution for moving structures (cardiac, fetal). Reducing depth, lines, or sector width improves it.

stem: What is the purpose of the backing material in a transducer?
options: To absorb sound waves traveling backward from the element, reducing pulse length and improving axial resolution
To transmit sound into the patient
rationale: Backing material (damping block) absorbs rearward-directed sound and dampens element vibration, shortening the spatial pulse length and improving axial (but not lateral) resolution.

stem: Compound imaging improves image quality by:
options: Steering the beam from multiple angles and averaging to reduce speckle and artifacts
rationale: Spatial compound imaging fires beams from multiple angles, averaging the resulting images. This reduces speckle, fills in shadows, and improves tissue boundary definition.

stem: The Q factor of a transducer describes:
options: The ratio of operating frequency to bandwidth — high Q = narrow bandwidth = poor resolution
rationale: Q factor = operating frequency / bandwidth. Low Q (broad bandwidth) transducers produce short pulses with better axial resolution and are preferred for imaging.

stem: Grating lobes are caused by:
options: The regular spacing of elements in array transducers creating off-axis beams
rationale: Grating lobes are unwanted off-axis beams produced by the regularly spaced elements in array transducers. They can create artifacts by mapping off-axis echoes into the image.

stem: The dynamic range of an ultrasound system refers to:
options: The range of signal amplitudes (weakest to strongest) that can be processed and displayed
The range of frequencies the transducer can produce
The range of depths that can be imaged
The range of patient sizes that can be scanned
rationale: Dynamic range = ratio of largest to smallest signal the system can handle, measured in dB. Compression maps the wide range of echo amplitudes to the display grayscale.

stem: What happens when the speed of sound in tissue differs from the assumed 1540 m/s?
options: Structures may be displayed at incorrect depths (range error)
rationale: If actual sound speed differs from 1540 m/s, round-trip time calculations are incorrect, placing structures at wrong depths. This is common in fat (1450 m/s) and bone (4080 m/s).

stem: Frame rate is determined by:
options: The number of pulses per frame and the PRF — frame rate = PRF / lines per frame
rationale: Frame rate = PRF / number of lines per frame. Increasing depth (lower PRF) or increasing line density reduces frame rate.

stem: Continuous wave (CW) Doppler differs from pulsed wave by:
options: Using separate transmit and receive elements with no depth discrimination but no aliasing
Using one element for both functions
Being limited by the Nyquist limit
rationale: CW Doppler uses continuous transmission/reception with no aliasing (measures any velocity) but cannot determine the depth of the signal source (range ambiguity).

stem: The purpose of an acoustic lens on a transducer is:
options: To focus the beam in the elevation plane
To protect the patient from ultrasound
rationale: The acoustic lens focuses the beam in the elevation (out-of-plane) plane to improve slice thickness. Electronic focusing handles the in-plane (lateral) direction.

stem: Mirror image artifact occurs:
options: When a strong reflector (like the diaphragm) creates a duplicate image of a real structure on the opposite side
rationale: Mirror artifact: the diaphragm acts as a specular reflector, creating a duplicated image of structures (often liver masses) appearing to be above the diaphragm in the thorax.

stem: Refraction artifact occurs when:
options: The sound beam changes direction at an interface between tissues with different speeds of sound
The beam hits a perfect reflector
rationale: Refraction (Snell's law) bends the beam at oblique interfaces where sound speeds differ, causing lateral misregistration of structures.

stem: Edge shadowing (refractile shadowing) is seen at:
options: The curved edges of cysts, vessels, and other round structures
rationale: Edge shadowing occurs at the curved margins of round structures (cysts, vessels) due to refraction. Beams hitting the curved edge are refracted away, creating narrow shadows.

stem: The B-mode display converts:
options: Returned echo amplitude into brightness on the display
rationale: B-mode (brightness mode) displays echo amplitude as varying brightness levels on a 2D grayscale image. Stronger echoes = brighter dots. It's the standard imaging mode.

stem: M-mode (motion mode) is particularly useful for:
options: Evaluating rapidly moving structures like cardiac valves and fetal heart
rationale: M-mode plots a single beam line over time, showing motion patterns. It has excellent temporal resolution, ideal for cardiac valve motion, fetal heart rate, and pneumothorax detection.

stem: The normal common hepatic duct diameter should be less than:
options: 4 mm (or up to 6 mm in older adults)
rationale: Normal CHD <4 mm. Normal CBD <6-7 mm. Intrahepatic ducts normally not visible. Dilation = obstruction until proven otherwise.

stem: Focal fatty infiltration of the liver typically appears as:
options: A geographic hyperechoic area without mass effect on vessels or bile ducts
A round hypoechoic mass with mass effect
A cystic lesion with posterior enhancement
rationale: Focal fatty infiltration appears hyperechoic with angular/geographic margins, has NO mass effect on adjacent vessels, and is found in typical locations (periportal, perihilum).

stem: Focal fatty sparing in the liver appears as:
options: A relatively hypoechoic area in an otherwise diffusely fatty liver, without mass effect
rationale: Focal fatty sparing: relatively hypoechoic area in a diffusely fatty (bright) liver. Found in typical locations (adjacent to GB fossa, near portal vein). No mass effect.

stem: The Couinaud classification divides the liver into:
options: 8 functionally independent segments based on portal and hepatic vein anatomy
rationale: Couinaud classification: 8 segments (I-VIII) based on portal vein branches and hepatic veins. Segment I = caudate lobe. Important for surgical planning.

stem: The normal aortic diameter in adults should be less than:
rationale: Normal abdominal aorta <2.0 cm. Ectatic: 2.0-3.0 cm. Aneurysm: >3.0 cm. Measurement should be outer wall to outer wall in AP dimension.

stem: The pancreatic duct (Wirsung) normally measures:
options: Less than 2-3 mm in the body
rationale: Normal main pancreatic duct ≤2-3 mm. Dilation suggests downstream obstruction (stone, mass). The duct courses through the body/tail and joins the CBD at the ampulla of Vater.

stem: Splenomegaly is defined as a spleen length greater than:
rationale: Splenomegaly: >13 cm in length. Causes include portal hypertension, infection (mono, malaria), hematologic disorders (lymphoma, leukemia), and storage diseases.

stem: The IVC normally demonstrates respiratory variation with:
options: Collapse during inspiration due to decreased intrathoracic pressure
rationale: Normal IVC collapses partially with inspiration (negative intrathoracic pressure draws blood from IVC into right atrium). Lack of collapse suggests elevated right atrial pressure.

stem: Hepatic steatosis grading on ultrasound: Grade 1 is characterized by:
options: Mildly increased liver echogenicity with normal visualization of diaphragm and hepatic vessels
Marked increase with poor diaphragm visualization
rationale: Grade 1 (mild): slight increase in echogenicity with normal vessel visualization. Grade 2: vessels obscured. Grade 3: diaphragm and deep structures not visible.

stem: The psoas muscle is located:
options: Retroperitoneally along the lateral aspect of the lumbar spine
rationale: The psoas muscle lies retroperitoneally along the lumbar spine. It serves as an important landmark and can harbor abscesses, hematomas, or tumors.

stem: A Klatskin tumor is:
options: A cholangiocarcinoma at the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts
rationale: Klatskin tumor: cholangiocarcinoma at the hepatic duct bifurcation. US shows intrahepatic bile duct dilation with normal-size extrahepatic duct. The mass may be difficult to visualize.

stem: Nutcracker phenomenon on ultrasound shows:
options: Compression of the left renal vein between the SMA and aorta with proximal dilation
rationale: The nutcracker: LRV compressed between SMA and aorta causing proximal LRV dilation and increased velocity at the narrowing. Can cause hematuria and pelvic congestion.

stem: Sonographic features of acute cholecystitis include:
options: Gallstones, positive Murphy's sign, GB wall thickening >3 mm, and pericholecystic fluid
rationale: Acute cholecystitis: gallstones (usually impacted in cystic duct), positive sonographic Murphy's sign, wall thickening >3 mm, and pericholecystic fluid. Sensitivity ~88%.

stem: Porcelain gallbladder on ultrasound shows:
options: Calcification of the gallbladder wall with posterior acoustic shadowing
rationale: Porcelain gallbladder: calcification in the GB wall creating a curvilinear echogenic structure with dense posterior shadowing. Associated with increased risk of gallbladder carcinoma.

stem: The 'double duct sign' refers to:
options: Simultaneous dilation of both the common bile duct and pancreatic duct, suggesting pancreatic head pathology
rationale: Double duct sign: dilated CBD and pancreatic duct, typically from pancreatic head mass (usually malignancy) obstructing both ducts. Requires further imaging (CT/MRI/ERCP).

stem: Anembryonic pregnancy (blighted ovum) on ultrasound shows:
options: A gestational sac ≥25 mm MSD without a visible embryo
A normal embryo within the sac
rationale: Anembryonic pregnancy: empty gestational sac with MSD ≥25 mm by TVUS without a visible embryo. Discriminatory MSD for diagnosis is 25 mm (TVUS).

stem: The double decidual sac sign helps identify:
options: An intrauterine gestational sac versus a pseudogestational sac of ectopic pregnancy
rationale: The double decidual sac sign shows two concentric echogenic rings (decidua capsularis and parietalis) surrounding the early gestational sac, confirming intrauterine location.

stem: Cervical length measurement is performed to assess:
options: Risk of preterm birth — length <25 mm at 16-24 weeks is concerning
rationale: Cervical length <25 mm before 24 weeks is associated with increased preterm birth risk. Measured by TVUS from internal to external os.

stem: The normal umbilical cord contains:
options: Two arteries and one vein (three vessels)
rationale: Normal cord: 2 arteries + 1 vein = 3 vessels. A single umbilical artery (2-vessel cord) is associated with renal anomalies, cardiac defects, and IUGR.

stem: The most common cause of first trimester bleeding is:
rationale: Most common cause of first trimester bleeding: threatened miscarriage or subchorionic hemorrhage. US evaluation determines viability, location, and any subchorionic hematoma.

stem: Fetal hydrocephalus on ultrasound is characterized by:
options: Dilation of the lateral ventricles >10 mm (ventriculomegaly)
rationale: Ventriculomegaly: lateral ventricle atrium measures >10 mm. Mild: 10-12 mm. Moderate: 12-15 mm. Severe: >15 mm. Causes include aqueductal stenosis, neural tube defects, and infection.

stem: Placental abruption on ultrasound may show:
options: Retroplacental or subchorionic hematoma, though US sensitivity is limited (only 25-50%)
rationale: Placental abruption: retroplacental, marginal, or subchorionic clot. US has LIMITED sensitivity (25-50%) — clinical presentation may be more reliable. Fresh blood may be isoechoic to placenta.

stem: The femur length-to-abdominal circumference ratio (FL/AC) is used to:
options: Assess symmetry of fetal growth — asymmetric IUGR shows elevated FL/AC ratio
rationale: FL/AC ratio: a high ratio (>23.5) suggests asymmetric IUGR where the head/femur are spared but abdominal growth lags (brain-sparing effect). Normal ~22.

stem: Echogenic bowel in the fetus is associated with:
options: Chromosomal abnormalities (trisomy 21), cystic fibrosis, fetal infection, or swallowed blood
rationale: Echogenic bowel (bright as bone) is a soft marker. Associations: Down syndrome, CF, CMV/toxoplasmosis infection, GI atresia, and swallowed blood (no clinical significance).

stem: The middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV) is used to assess:
options: Fetal anemia — elevated MCA-PSV indicates compensatory increased cardiac output
rationale: Elevated MCA-PSV (>1.5 MoM) detects fetal anemia non-invasively. Used in Rh isoimmunization, parvovirus B19, and TTTS. It has largely replaced invasive amniocentesis for anemia screening.

stem: Normal umbilical artery Doppler flow in the third trimester shows:
options: Forward end-diastolic flow throughout the cardiac cycle
rationale: Normal UA Doppler shows continuous forward flow in diastole. Absent or reversed end-diastolic flow indicates significantly increased placental resistance and is associated with poor fetal outcomes.

stem: A choroid plexus cyst in the fetus:
options: Is a common normal variant, usually resolving by 26 weeks, but in isolation is a weak marker for trisomy 18
rationale: CPCs are found in 1-2% of 2nd trimester fetuses. Isolated CPCs have a very low risk of aneuploidy. Associated anomalies or maternal age significantly increase risk.

stem: Normal common carotid artery (CCA) intima-media thickness (IMT) should be:
rationale: Normal CCA IMT <1.0 mm. Values >1.0 mm are associated with increased cardiovascular risk. Measured on the far wall as the double-line pattern.

stem: External carotid artery (ECA) is identified by:
options: Temporal tap response (oscillation on spectral Doppler), high-resistance waveform, and branches
No flow response to temporal tapping
rationale: ECA identification: temporal tap causes oscillations in the spectral waveform (temporal artery is an ECA branch), high-resistance waveform, smaller caliber, has branches.

stem: The internal carotid artery (ICA) typically has:
options: A low-resistance waveform with continuous forward diastolic flow and no extracranial branches
Higher position at the bifurcation always
rationale: ICA: low-resistance waveform (supplies brain = low resistance). Continuous diastolic flow, no branches in the neck, usually posterolateral at the bifurcation, often has a bulb.

stem: Chronic DVT on ultrasound may show:
options: Echogenic, organized thrombus with partial recanalization, wall thickening, and incompetent valves
rationale: Chronic DVT shows echogenic/organized thrombus, contracted vein with wall thickening, possible recanalization channels, venous reflux from valve damage.

stem: A pseudoaneurysm (PSA) on ultrasound shows:
options: A contained extravascular collection with swirling 'to-and-fro' flow in the neck on Doppler
A true aneurysm of the artery
rationale: PSA: contained hematoma communicating with an artery through a neck/tract. Characteristic 'yin-yang' swirling pattern in the sac and 'to-and-fro' flow in the neck.

stem: Carotid body tumor (paraganglioma) on ultrasound appears as:
options: A hypervascular solid mass at the carotid bifurcation splaying the ICA and ECA apart
rationale: Carotid body tumors are highly vascular solid masses at the carotid bifurcation that characteristically splay (widen) the distance between the ICA and ECA.

stem: Portal vein thrombosis on ultrasound shows:
options: Echogenic material within the portal vein lumen, absent or partial color flow, and possible cavernous transformation
rationale: Portal vein thrombosis: intraluminal echogenic material, absent/reduced color fill, possible collateral vessels (cavernous transformation), and hepatofugal flow in surrounding vessels.

stem: Hepatofugal portal venous flow means:
options: Flow direction is away from the liver (reversed), indicating portal hypertension
rationale: Hepatofugal = reversed portal flow (away from liver). Normal flow is hepatopetal (toward liver). Reversal indicates significant portal hypertension.

stem: The Valsalva maneuver in venous assessment is used to:
options: Evaluate venous valve competence by increasing abdominal pressure and checking for reflux
rationale: Valsalva increases abdominal pressure, temporarily stopping or reversing venous flow. Competent valves prevent retrograde flow. Reflux >0.5 seconds indicates incompetence.

stem: Spectral broadening in a carotid artery waveform indicates:
options: Turbulent flow suggesting stenosis or post-stenotic turbulence
rationale: Spectral broadening: filling in of the spectral window due to a wide range of velocities (turbulence). It's an early sign of hemodynamically significant stenosis.

stem: Nabothian cysts on ultrasound are:
options: Benign retention cysts of the cervix appearing as small anechoic cysts — a normal finding
rationale: Nabothian cysts are mucous retention cysts in the cervix. They appear as small, round, anechoic structures. They are normal findings requiring no treatment.

stem: The secretory (luteal) phase endometrium appears:
rationale: In the secretory phase, progesterone from the corpus luteum makes the endometrium thick (10-14 mm), echogenic, and homogeneous in preparation for implantation.

stem: An endometrial polyp on ultrasound may show:
options: A focal echogenic mass within the endometrial cavity, best seen with saline infusion sonohysterography
rationale: Endometrial polyps appear as echogenic focal masses within the cavity. They may contain a feeding vessel. SIS (saline infusion) enhances detection by outlining the polyp with fluid.

stem: Ovarian torsion on ultrasound may show:
options: Enlarged ovary with peripheral follicles, absent or decreased Doppler flow, and free fluid
rationale: Ovarian torsion: enlarged edematous ovary, peripheral follicles ('string of pearls'), absent/decreased arterial/venous flow (though flow may be preserved early), free fluid, and a whirlpool sign of twisted pedicle.

stem: A corpus luteum cyst on ultrasound appears as:
options: A thick-walled, hypoechoic to echogenic cyst with peripheral 'ring of fire' vascularity on Doppler
A completely anechoic simple cyst with thin walls
rationale: Corpus luteum cysts have thick walls, variable internal echogenicity, and characteristic 'ring of fire' peripheral vascularity. They are normal physiologic structures in reproductive-age women.

stem: Sonohysterography (SIS) is performed by:
options: Instilling sterile saline into the endometrial cavity through a catheter during transvaginal ultrasound
rationale: SIS instills saline into the uterine cavity, distending it to better evaluate intracavitary pathology (polyps, submucosal fibroids, adhesions). Performed in proliferative phase.

stem: A hemorrhagic cyst on ultrasound typically shows:
options: Complex internal echoes with reticular (lace-like) or fibrin strand pattern, no solid nodularity or internal vascularity
rationale: Hemorrhagic cysts contain fibrin strands creating a reticular pattern. They lack solid nodules and internal flow. Most resolve spontaneously and should be followed to confirm resolution.

stem: A mature follicle ready for ovulation measures approximately:
rationale: A dominant follicle reaches 18-25 mm before ovulation. Growth rate is ~1-3 mm/day in the late follicular phase. Follicles <10 mm are considered antral follicles.

stem: A thyroid nodule that is taller than wide (AP dimension > transverse) is suspicious because:
options: This shape is more commonly seen in malignant nodules growing across tissue planes
Shape is irrelevant in thyroid assessment
rationale: Taller-than-wide orientation is a suspicious feature associated with malignancy. Benign nodules tend to grow along tissue planes (wider than tall).

stem: Hashimoto's thyroiditis on ultrasound typically shows:
options: Diffusely heterogeneous, hypoechoic thyroid parenchyma with echogenic septations creating a 'micronodular' pattern
rationale: Hashimoto's: diffusely hypoechoic, heterogeneous parenchyma with fibrous septations. May show 'micronodular' or 'giraffe skin' pattern. Associated with hypothyroidism.

stem: An epididymitis on ultrasound shows:
options: Enlarged, hypoechoic epididymis with increased vascularity on color Doppler
Normal epididymis with no flow changes
rationale: Acute epididymitis: enlarged, hypoechoic epididymis with markedly increased color Doppler flow (hyperemia). May have reactive hydrocele and scrotal wall thickening.

stem: A varicocele is defined as:
options: Dilated pampiniform plexus veins (>3 mm) that increase with Valsalva maneuver
rationale: Varicocele: dilated pampiniform veins (>3 mm, typically >2 mm) with retrograde flow on Valsalva. More common on the left side. May affect fertility.

stem: A hydrocele on ultrasound appears as:
options: Anechoic fluid collection surrounding the testis between the visceral and parietal tunica vaginalis
rationale: Simple hydrocele: anechoic fluid surrounding the testis. Complex hydrocele may contain echogenic debris, septations, or loculations (infection, trauma, or chronic).

stem: The normal adult thyroid lobe measures approximately:
options: 4-6 cm length, 1.5-2 cm AP, 2-3 cm transverse
rationale: Normal thyroid lobe: 4-6 cm length, 1.5-2 cm AP, 2-3 cm transverse. Isthmus: <3 mm. Total volume: 10-15 mL (female) to 12-18 mL (male).

stem: Breast BI-RADS 3 assessment indicates:
options: Probably benign — short-interval follow-up (6 months) recommended
rationale: BI-RADS 3: probably benign (<2% malignancy risk). Short-interval follow-up (6 months) recommended to document stability. Examples: non-palpable solid mass with benign features.

stem: Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) on ultrasound shows:
options: Thickened, hypoechoic common extensor tendon origin with possible tearing and neovascularity at the lateral epicondyle
rationale: Tennis elbow: thickened, hypoechoic common extensor tendon (especially ECRB) at its origin. May show partial tearing, calcification, and neovascularity on power Doppler.

stem: A full-thickness Achilles tendon tear on ultrasound shows:
options: Complete disruption of the tendon with a gap, retracted ends, and Thompson test positivity
rationale: Complete Achilles tear: full-thickness gap with retracted tendon stumps. The gap may fill with hematoma (hypoechoic). Dynamic scanning confirms lack of tendon excursion.

stem: Plantar fasciitis on ultrasound is diagnosed when:
options: The plantar fascia at the calcaneal insertion is thickened >4 mm, hypoechoic, and may show loss of fibrillar pattern
The plantar fascia is thin and hyperechoic
There is no visible plantar fascia
rationale: Plantar fasciitis: fascial thickness >4 mm at calcaneal insertion, hypoechoic edema, loss of fibrillar pattern. Heel spur may be present but is not diagnostic.

stem: Ultrasound of a joint effusion shows:
options: Anechoic or hypoechoic fluid distending the joint capsule beyond its normal limits
rationale: Joint effusion: fluid (anechoic in simple, echogenic in complex/hemorrhagic) distends the joint recess beyond normal. Comparison with contralateral joint helps confirm.

stem: De Quervain's tenosynovitis involves:
options: The first dorsal compartment tendons (APL and EPB) at the radial styloid
The flexor tendons of the fingers
rationale: De Quervain's: tendinopathy/tenosynovitis of the 1st dorsal compartment (abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis). US shows thickened tendons with fluid in the tendon sheath.

stem: The 'comet tail' artifact from cholesterol crystals in the gallbladder:
options: Differentiates cholesterol crystals/adenomyomatosis from gallstones, as stones produce clean shadowing
rationale: Comet tail artifacts (V-shaped reverberations) arise from cholesterol crystals embedded in the GB wall (adenomyomatosis) or small cholesterol polyps. Unlike stones, they do not shadow cleanly.

stem: A 'target lesion' in the liver on ultrasound suggests:
options: Metastatic disease with a hypoechoic halo surrounding an echogenic center
rationale: Target lesions (bulls-eye/halo pattern): echogenic center with hypoechoic rim. Common in hepatic metastases. The halo represents compressed liver tissue or peripheral tumor growth.

stem: 'Winking owl sign' on ultrasound of the spine suggests:
options: A vertebral body metastasis destroying one pedicle on axial imaging
rationale: On axial spine imaging, normal vertebrae show two echogenic pedicles (owl's eyes). Destruction of one pedicle by metastasis creates the 'winking owl' — one eye is missing.

stem: The 'ring down artifact' is caused by:
options: Resonance of sound within a fluid-air interface creating a series of parallel bands extending from the surface
Strong reflections from a solid surface
rationale: Ring down artifact: continuous series of echoes extending from a fluid-air interface (e.g., bowel gas). It differs from comet tail in that it doesn't diminish in intensity as rapidly.

stem: Increased hepatorenal echogenicity ratio indicates:
options: Hepatic steatosis (fatty liver) — the liver appears brighter than the adjacent right kidney cortex
rationale: Normal liver is slightly more echogenic than kidney cortex. Increased hepatorenal contrast (much brighter liver) indicates hepatic steatosis. This is a semi-quantitative assessment.

stem: A 'dirty shadow' posterior to a structure suggests:
options: Gas-containing structure (bowel) rather than calcification (which produces clean shadow)
rationale: Dirty shadowing (echogenic shadow with internal echoes) = gas. Clean shadowing (anechoic, sharp-edged) = calcification or bone. This distinction aids in differential diagnosis.

stem: Hepatic metastases may appear as:
options: Variable echogenicity — hypoechoic, hyperechoic, mixed, or target pattern depending on primary tumor
rationale: Hepatic metastases have variable appearances. Colon CA mets tend to be echogenic. Lymphoma mets are hypoechoic. Breast/lung mets may show target pattern. Calcified mets from mucinous tumors.

stem: Testicular microlithiasis on ultrasound shows:
options: Multiple (≥5) tiny echogenic foci without shadowing scattered throughout the testicular parenchyma
rationale: Testicular microlithiasis: ≥5 non-shadowing echogenic foci per field. Associated with increased risk of testicular germ cell tumors. Annual follow-up recommended.

stem: A 'snowstorm' pattern in the uterus may indicate:
rationale: The 'snowstorm' or 'cluster of grapes' pattern shows multiple small cystic spaces filling the uterine cavity, characteristic of complete hydatidiform mole. β-hCG is markedly elevated.

stem: Splenunculus (accessory spleen) on ultrasound appears as:
options: A small round structure with echogenicity identical to the spleen, usually near the splenic hilum
A calcified mass near the spleen
rationale: Accessory spleens are found in ~10% of population. They are round, isoechoic to the spleen, typically <2 cm, near the splenic hilum. Important not to confuse with lymph nodes or masses.

stem: Pneumobilia on ultrasound appears as:
options: Bright echogenic foci with ring-down artifact in the bile ducts, predominantly in the non-dependent portion
rationale: Pneumobilia: air in the biliary system creating bright echogenic foci with ring-down artifacts. Moves to non-dependent position. Causes: prior sphincterotomy, biliary-enteric fistula, or infection.

stem: Doppler angle of 0 degrees produces:
rationale: At 0° (parallel to flow), cosine = 1, giving maximum Doppler shift. At 90° (perpendicular), cosine = 0, giving no shift. Ideal angle is <60°.

stem: The Doppler angle should ideally be maintained at:
options: Less than 60 degrees for accurate velocity measurements
rationale: Angles <60° minimize velocity measurement errors. At >60°, small angle changes cause large velocity errors. At 90°, no Doppler shift is detected.

stem: What is the 3 dB point in ultrasound?
options: The half-power point — where intensity drops to 50% of maximum, defining beam width
The point where all sound is absorbed
rationale: The -3 dB point = 50% intensity reduction. It defines beam width and sensitivity boundaries. The -6 dB point = 25% intensity.

stem: Acoustic output is regulated by:
options: The FDA, which sets maximum intensity limits (derated SPTA ≤720 mW/cm²)
rationale: The FDA regulates acoustic output with limits: SPTA ≤720 mW/cm² for most applications, MI ≤1.9, TI should be minimized. The AIUM provides additional guidelines.

stem: Color Doppler assigns colors based on:
options: The direction and mean velocity of flow relative to the transducer
The amplitude of the signal only
rationale: Color Doppler maps flow direction (typically red toward, blue away from transducer — BART) and mean velocity (lighter shades = faster). It uses autocorrelation technique.

stem: Elastography in ultrasound measures:
options: Tissue stiffness by evaluating deformation or shear wave speed through tissue
rationale: Elastography assesses tissue stiffness. Strain elastography measures tissue displacement under compression. Shear wave elastography measures shear wave velocity (stiffer = faster).

stem: What causes the 'twinkling artifact' on color Doppler?
options: A rapidly alternating color signal behind strongly reflecting, irregular surfaces like kidney stones
rationale: Twinkling artifact produces random color mosaic behind rough, strongly reflecting surfaces (stones, calcifications). It's useful for detecting small renal/ureteral stones.

stem: Echinococcal (hydatid) cyst of the liver on ultrasound may show:
options: A cyst with internal daughter cysts, floating membranes, and calcification of the wall (water lily sign)
rationale: Hydatid cysts show complex internal architecture: daughter cysts, floating membranes (water lily sign), internal debris (hydatid sand), and wall calcification. Caused by Echinococcus.

stem: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) on ultrasound may appear as:
options: A variable echogenicity mass (often hypoechoic when small, mixed when large) with increased vascularity
rationale: HCC has variable US appearance. Small HCCs are often hypoechoic. Larger tumors become heterogeneous. They may show a peripheral hypoechoic halo. Color Doppler shows arterial feeding vessels.

stem: The 'starry sky' pattern of the spleen suggests:
options: Infection, lymphoma, or other infiltrative process causing diffuse hyperechoic foci
rationale: Splenic 'starry sky': diffuse hyperechoic foci throughout the parenchyma. Seen in infection, lymphoma, sarcoidosis, and storage diseases.

stem: Acute appendicitis on ultrasound shows:
options: A non-compressible, blind-ending tubular structure >6 mm in diameter with periappendiceal inflammation
rationale: Acute appendicitis: non-compressible appendix >6 mm diameter, periappendiceal fat inflammation, possible appendicolith (calcified), and increased color flow. Graded compression technique.

stem: The normal IVC diameter changes with respiration:
options: Decreases with inspiration (sniff test) — normal IVC collapse >50%
rationale: Normal IVC shows >50% collapse with sniff test. Plethoric IVC (<50% collapse) suggests elevated right atrial pressure. IVC assessment helps estimate right atrial pressure.

stem: Xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis on ultrasound:
options: May mimic gallbladder carcinoma with wall thickening, intramural nodules, and loss of wall layers
Always has a porcelain gallbladder appearance
rationale: XGC mimics GB carcinoma: marked wall thickening, intramural hypoechoic nodules, disruption of wall layers, and possible extension to liver. Pathology shows lipid-laden macrophages.

stem: Wandering spleen on ultrasound is characterized by:
options: Absence of the spleen in its normal LUQ location with an ectopic pelvic or abdominal mass of splenic echogenicity
rationale: Wandering spleen: congenitally lax or absent splenic ligaments allow the spleen to migrate. Risk of torsion. Found ectopically with echogenicity matching normal spleen.

stem: Hepatic adenoma on ultrasound typically appears as:
options: A well-defined solid mass, often hyperechoic, most common in women on oral contraceptives
rationale: Hepatic adenomas are well-defined, often hyperechoic solid masses associated with OCP use. They have hemorrhage risk. Larger ones may show heterogeneous echotexture from hemorrhage/necrosis.

stem: Choroid plexus location in fetal ultrasound:
options: Within the lateral ventricles — they produce cerebrospinal fluid
rationale: Choroid plexus lines the lateral ventricles and produces CSF. Normally echogenic and prominent. Ventriculomegaly causes the choroid to 'dangle' away from the medial wall.

stem: The cisterna magna measurement should be:
options: 2-10 mm (>10 mm may indicate Dandy-Walker malformation, <2 mm may indicate Chiari malformation)
rationale: Normal cisterna magna: 2-10 mm. Enlarged (>10 mm): Dandy-Walker variant/malformation, mega cisterna magna. Obliterated (<2 mm): Chiari II malformation.

stem: A two-vessel cord (single umbilical artery) is associated with:
options: Renal anomalies, cardiac defects, and IUGR
rationale: Single umbilical artery (2-vessel cord) occurs in 0.5-1% of pregnancies. Associated with renal anomalies, cardiac defects, chromosomal abnormalities, and IUGR. Merits detailed anomaly scan.

stem: Fetal gastroschisis on ultrasound shows:
options: Free-floating bowel herniating through a paraumbilical abdominal wall defect (usually right of umbilicus)
Bowel within a membrane-covered sac at the umbilicus
rationale: Gastroschisis: bowel herniates through a defect to the RIGHT of the umbilical cord insertion. No covering membrane. Not typically associated with chromosomal abnormalities.

stem: Fetal omphalocele on ultrasound shows:
options: Herniated abdominal contents within a peritoneal membrane at the umbilical cord insertion
rationale: Omphalocele: membrane-covered herniation at the cord insertion. May contain bowel and/or liver. Associated with chromosomal abnormalities (especially trisomy 18) and cardiac defects.

stem: The S/D ratio of the umbilical artery decreases during normal pregnancy because:
options: Placental vascular resistance decreases with advancing gestation as the placenta matures
rationale: Normal UA S/D ratio decreases throughout pregnancy as placental vascular resistance falls. Typical values: 4.0 at 20 weeks to <3.0 at term. Elevated ratios suggest placental insufficiency.

stem: Neural tube defects on ultrasound are associated with:
options: Lemon sign (frontal bone scalloping) and banana sign (cerebellar compression) in the fetal head
rationale: Open spina bifida causes Chiari II malformation. US shows lemon sign (scalloped frontal bones), banana sign (compressed cerebellum), and ventriculomegaly. Direct spine visualization confirms.

stem: What is the purpose of the Doppler ductus venosus assessment?
options: Evaluating fetal cardiac function and right heart compliance — abnormal a-wave indicates fetal compromise
rationale: Ductus venosus Doppler assesses fetal cardiac function. Absent or reversed a-wave indicates increased right atrial pressure, associated with cardiac failure, aneuploidy, and poor outcome.

stem: Macrosomia is defined as estimated fetal weight:
rationale: Macrosomia: EFW >4000-4500g. Risk factors: gestational diabetes, maternal obesity, prolonged pregnancy. US accuracy for macrosomia is limited (±15% error in weight estimation).

stem: The string sign in carotid ultrasound refers to:
options: A thin residual lumen in a critically stenosed (>95%) internal carotid artery
rationale: The string sign: a barely patent, thread-like residual lumen in critical ICA stenosis (>95%). Extremely high velocities may paradoxically decrease as flow volume drops.

stem: May-Thurner syndrome involves:
options: Compression of the left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery, predisposing to left leg DVT
rationale: May-Thurner: right CIA compresses left CIV against the spine. This anatomic compression predisposes to left iliofemoral DVT, especially in young women.

stem: Continuous venous flow (loss of phasicity) in lower extremity veins suggests:
rationale: Loss of respiratory phasicity (continuous flow) in lower extremity veins suggests proximal obstruction (iliac vein or IVC thrombus) preventing normal respiratory variation.

stem: Peak systolic velocity >200 cm/s in the renal artery suggests:
rationale: Renal artery PSV >200 cm/s or renal-to-aortic ratio (RAR) >3.5 suggests ≥60% stenosis. Additional criteria: post-stenotic turbulence and tardus-parvus waveform.

stem: Thoracic outlet syndrome on vascular ultrasound shows:
options: Positional compression of the subclavian artery or vein with provocative maneuvers (arms raised)
rationale: Vascular TOS: compression of subclavian vessels during provocative maneuvers (arm abduction/external rotation). US shows flow acceleration or cessation with positional changes.

stem: A carotid dissection on ultrasound may show:
options: Intimal flap, double lumen, mural hematoma, or tapered narrowing of the ICA
rationale: Carotid dissection: hypoechoic mural hematoma narrowing the lumen, possible intimal flap creating double lumen, and tapered narrowing. CTA/MRA confirms diagnosis.

stem: Asherman syndrome on ultrasound may show:
options: Intrauterine adhesions (synechiae) with thin or absent endometrium and distortion of the cavity
rationale: Asherman syndrome: intrauterine adhesions causing partial or complete cavity obliteration. US may show thin endometrium, hyperechoic bands, and poor cavity visualization. SIS confirms.

stem: Cervical ectopic pregnancy on ultrasound shows:
options: Gestational sac or trophoblastic tissue within the cervical canal with empty uterine cavity
rationale: Cervical ectopic: gestational sac within the cervix with closed internal os and empty uterine cavity. The 'hourglass' uterus sign may be seen. This is a rare but serious emergency.

stem: A pedunculated fibroid may mimic:
options: An adnexal mass (ovarian tumor) when the pedicle is not visualized
rationale: Pedunculated subserosal fibroids can simulate adnexal masses if the uterine stalk connection is not identified. Color Doppler showing bridging vessels helps confirm uterine origin.

stem: The normal postmenopausal ovary:
options: Is small and atrophic, often difficult to visualize, measuring <2 mL volume
Is the same size as premenopausal
rationale: Postmenopausal ovaries are small (<2 mL), homogeneous, and may not be visualized on US. Small simple cysts (<1 cm) may persist and are typically benign.

stem: Fine needle aspiration (FNA) of thyroid nodules is typically recommended for:
options: Suspicious nodules ≥1 cm or nodules with highly suspicious features at smaller sizes per ACR TI-RADS
All thyroid nodules regardless of size
rationale: TI-RADS guides FNA recommendations based on point score and size: TR5 (highly suspicious) ≥1 cm, TR4 ≥1.5 cm, TR3 ≥2.5 cm. Smaller thresholds for follow-up.

stem: A lymph node suspicious for malignancy on ultrasound shows:
options: Round shape, loss of fatty hilum, cortical thickening, irregular borders, and increased vascularity
Oval shape with preserved fatty hilum
rationale: Suspicious nodes: round shape (L/S ratio <2), absent/displaced hilum, cortical thickening, heterogeneity, peripheral/mixed vascularity, and irregular borders.

stem: Salivary gland calculi (sialolithiasis) on ultrasound shows:
options: Echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing within or along the duct, with proximal duct dilation
rationale: Salivary stones: echogenic foci with clean acoustic shadowing. Proximal duct dilation may be present. Submandibular gland is most commonly affected.

stem: The normal median nerve at the wrist on ultrasound appears:
options: Honeycomb pattern on cross-section (fascicular pattern) with echogenic epineurium
rationale: Normal nerves show a characteristic fascicular or honeycomb pattern on cross-section: hypoechoic fascicles surrounded by hyperechoic perineurium. Normal median nerve CSA <10 mm².

stem: Trigger finger on ultrasound shows:
options: Thickened A1 pulley with difficulty of tendon gliding and possible tendon nodule
rationale: Trigger finger: thickened A1 pulley (>0.5 mm) restricting flexor tendon gliding. A tendon nodule may catch at the pulley. Dynamic scanning shows snapping during finger flexion/extension.

stem: Muscle strain on ultrasound may show:
options: Hypoechoic/anechoic area within the muscle fibers indicating edema or hemorrhage at the musculotendinous junction
rationale: Muscle strains show hypoechoic/anechoic areas at the musculotendinous junction. Grade 1: edema without fiber disruption. Grade 2: partial tear. Grade 3: complete rupture.

stem: Morton's neuroma on ultrasound appears as:
options: A hypoechoic oval mass in the intermetatarsal space, typically between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal heads
rationale: Morton's neuroma: hypoechoic, ovoid lesion in the intermetatarsal space, most common between 3rd-4th MT heads. Mulder's click test may be performed under dynamic scanning.

stem: Calcific tendinitis on ultrasound shows:
options: Echogenic focus within the tendon with variable shadowing, often in the supraspinatus tendon
rationale: Calcific tendinitis: echogenic deposit within the tendon (most common in supraspinatus). May shadow. Acute phase: softer/less echogenic. Chronic: well-defined with shadowing.

stem: 'Ring sign' on transverse view of the neck suggests:
options: An ectopic pregnancy in the fallopian tube showing a tubal ring
rationale: The tubal ring sign in ectopic pregnancy shows an echogenic ring (trophoblast) surrounding a gestational sac in the adnexa, separate from the ovary.

stem: Hepatic vein flow pattern in tricuspid regurgitation shows:
options: Systolic flow reversal in the hepatic veins
rationale: Significant TR causes hepatic vein systolic flow reversal. Normal HV flow: systolic forward, diastolic forward, end-diastolic reversal (with atrial contraction).

stem: Porcelain gallbladder wall calcification is significant because:
options: It carries an increased risk of gallbladder carcinoma
rationale: Porcelain gallbladder (wall calcification) has historically been associated with increased gallbladder carcinoma risk. Prophylactic cholecystectomy may be recommended.

stem: A 'whirlpool sign' on color Doppler suggests:
options: Ovarian torsion or bowel volvulus — showing the twisted pedicle
rationale: The whirlpool sign shows a twisted vascular pedicle on color Doppler, characteristic of ovarian torsion. Also seen in testicular torsion and mesenteric volvulus.

stem: A 'sludge ball' in the gallbladder may mimic:
options: A mass or polyp, but it moves with positional changes unlike fixed lesions
rationale: Sludge balls (tumefactive sludge) are mobile, echogenic aggregates that can mimic masses. Unlike polyps, they shift with patient repositioning. Unlike stones, they may not shadow.

stem: Crohn's disease on ultrasound may show:
options: Bowel wall thickening >3 mm, increased vascularity, loss of wall stratification, and mesenteric fat wrapping
rationale: Crohn's: transmural bowel wall thickening, hyperemia, loss of stratification, creeping fat, fistulas, and abscess formation. US can monitor disease activity and complications.

stem: The pseudomass effect in hepatic sonography occurs when:
options: Normal liver tissue adjacent to the gallbladder fossa or falciform ligament has different echogenicity, simulating a mass
A true hepatic mass is present
rationale: Pseudomasses: normal liver areas with different echogenicity due to focal fatty sparing, focal fatty infiltration, or caudate lobe variation. They show no mass effect on vessels.

stem: A splenic infarct on ultrasound appears as:
options: A peripheral wedge-shaped hypoechoic or heterogeneous area
rationale: Splenic infarcts: peripheral, wedge-shaped, hypoechoic areas (base at capsule, apex toward hilum). Caused by embolism, sickle cell, vasculitis. May evolve to echogenic scar.

stem: Attenuation in soft tissue is approximately:
rationale: Average attenuation in soft tissue = 0.5 dB per cm per MHz. Higher frequency = more attenuation. Total attenuation = coefficient × path length × frequency.

stem: The bandwidth of a transducer determines:
options: The range of frequencies the transducer can emit and receive
rationale: Bandwidth = range of frequencies a transducer can produce. Broadband (wide) transducers have better axial resolution and allow harmonic imaging. Narrowband is used for CW Doppler.

stem: Intensity is measured in:
rationale: Intensity = power/area (W/cm²). It describes the concentration of energy in the beam. Important for bioeffects assessment. SPTA and SPTP are common intensity descriptors.

stem: What is the difference between absorption and scattering?
options: Absorption converts sound energy to heat; scattering redirects sound in multiple directions
rationale: Absorption: acoustic energy converted to thermal energy. Scattering: sound redirected in multiple directions by structures smaller than the wavelength. Both contribute to attenuation.

stem: Gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm) can be caused by:
options: Acute cholecystitis, ascites, hepatitis, heart failure, hypoalbuminemia, or portal hypertension
