rationale: GB wall thickening >3 mm has many causes: cholecystitis (most specific with stones + Murphy's sign), ascites, hepatitis, CHF, hypoalbuminemia, portal HTN, AIDS cholangiopathy.

stem: Normal renal cortical echogenicity compared to liver is:
options: Equal to or less echogenic than the liver
Much more echogenic than the spleen
rationale: Normal renal cortex is equal to or slightly less echogenic than liver. Increased cortical echogenicity (brighter than liver) suggests medical renal disease.

stem: A staghorn calculus on ultrasound shows:
options: A large echogenic structure filling the renal pelvis and extending into calyces with posterior shadowing
rationale: Staghorn calculi: large branching stones filling the renal pelvis and extending into calyces. They appear as a large central echogenic structure with dense posterior shadowing.

stem: The 'double bubble' sign in fetal ultrasound indicates:
options: Duodenal atresia — dilated stomach and proximal duodenum
rationale: The double bubble sign: two adjacent fluid-filled structures (stomach and dilated proximal duodenum) with no distal bowel gas. Associated with trisomy 21 (30% of cases).

stem: Fetal renal agenesis is associated with:
options: Severe oligohydramnios (Potter sequence) due to absent fetal urine production
rationale: Bilateral renal agenesis: lethal condition with severe oligohydramnios (no fetal urine), non-visualization of kidneys or bladder, pulmonary hypoplasia. Part of Potter sequence.

stem: Cleft lip on fetal ultrasound is best visualized:
options: On coronal and axial views of the fetal face, showing a defect in the upper lip
rationale: Cleft lip: a gap in the upper lip seen on coronal and axial face views. Cleft palate may coexist but is harder to visualize sonographically.

stem: Normal renal artery RI (Resistive Index) in adults is:
rationale: Normal renal artery RI <0.70. RI >0.70 suggests intrinsic renal disease. RI difference >0.05 between kidneys may indicate unilateral pathology.

stem: ICA occlusion on ultrasound shows:
options: Absent flow in the ICA with possible echogenic material filling the lumen
rationale: ICA occlusion: no detectable flow (color, power, or spectral), often with echogenic material in the lumen. The ECA may show 'internalization' (low-resistance waveform) as collateral pathway.

stem: The 'necklace sign' on ultrasound of the ovary refers to:
options: Peripheral follicles arranged around the ovarian cortex in polycystic ovary syndrome
rationale: The necklace sign (string of pearls): ≥12 small follicles (2-9 mm) arranged peripherally around the ovarian cortex, characteristic of PCOS.

stem: A simple parovarian cyst on ultrasound is:
options: An anechoic cyst adjacent to but separate from the ovary, located in the broad ligament
rationale: Parovarian cysts arise from Wolffian duct remnants in the broad ligament. They are simple, anechoic, thin-walled, and separate from the ovary. Generally benign.

stem: The normal uterine size in a reproductive-age woman is approximately:
options: 7-8 cm length, 4-5 cm AP, 5-6 cm width
rationale: Normal adult uterus: ~7-8 cm length, 4-5 cm AP, 5-6 cm width. Nulliparous is smaller. Multiparous is larger. Postmenopausal uterus atrophies to ~3-5 cm.

stem: Breast fibroadenoma on ultrasound typically appears:
options: Well-defined, oval, hypoechoic solid mass with smooth margins, wider than tall, and possible gentle lobulations
Spiculated hypoechoic mass with posterior shadowing
rationale: Fibroadenomas are the most common benign breast mass in young women. Well-defined, oval, hypoechoic, wider-than-tall, smooth margins. BI-RADS 3 if classic features.

stem: Invasive ductal carcinoma on ultrasound may show:
options: Irregular, hypoechoic solid mass with spiculated margins, taller than wide, and posterior acoustic shadowing
rationale: Invasive ductal carcinoma: irregular, hypoechoic, taller-than-wide, spiculated margins, posterior shadowing, architectural distortion. BI-RADS 5 (highly suspicious).

stem: Subacute thyroiditis (de Quervain's) on ultrasound shows:
options: Focal hypoechoic areas in the thyroid with decreased vascularity and possible gland enlargement
rationale: Subacute thyroiditis: focal or diffuse hypoechoic areas (inflammation), decreased vascularity (unlike Graves'), gland tenderness. Often preceded by viral illness.

stem: A seminoma on ultrasound typically appears as:
options: A well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechoic intratesticular mass
rationale: Seminoma: the most common testicular malignancy. Usually well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechoic. May show increased vascularity. Any hypoechoic intratesticular mass is suspicious.

stem: The supraspinatus tendon on ultrasound is evaluated in:
options: The modified Crass or Middleton position with the arm in internal rotation behind the back or at the hip
Arms at the sides in neutral position
rationale: Modified Crass: arm internally rotated with hand behind back or on ipsilateral hip. This moves the supraspinatus from under the acromion for evaluation.

stem: Joint gout on ultrasound shows:
options: Hyperechoic deposits on articular cartilage (double contour sign) and tophaceous deposits
rationale: Gout: hyperechoic urate crystal deposits on articular cartilage creating the 'double contour sign.' Tophi appear as hyperechoic, heterogeneous aggregates around joints.

stem: Hepatic abscess on ultrasound may appear as:
options: A complex cystic mass with thick walls, internal debris, and possible gas echoes
A simple anechoic cyst with thin walls
rationale: Hepatic abscesses: complex cystic masses with thick, irregular walls, internal echoes/debris, possible gas (echogenic with dirty shadow). Clinical context (fever, WBC) is essential.

stem: The 'drooping lily' sign on renal ultrasound suggests:
options: Duplicated collecting system with upper pole obstruction — the lower moiety droops
rationale: Drooping lily: the lower moiety of a duplex kidney appears to 'droop' when the upper pole is obstructed/dilated by an ectopic ureterocele. The upper pole may be poorly visualized.

stem: Fetal choroid plexus appears echogenic and fills the lateral ventricles, normally measuring:
options: Less than 10 mm at the atrial level
rationale: The lateral ventricle atrium normally measures ≤10 mm throughout gestation. This measurement is used to assess ventriculomegaly, regardless of gestational age.

stem: Fetal club foot (talipes equinovarus) on ultrasound shows:
options: The sole of the foot visible in the same plane as the tibia and fibula
Only foot measuring large for dates
rationale: Club foot: the foot is persistently turned inward (inverted and plantarflexed) so the sole is visualized in the same sagittal plane as the leg bones.

stem: Elevated MCA-PSV above 1.5 MoM suggests:
rationale: MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM (multiples of median) suggests moderate-severe fetal anemia. Used in Rh isoimmunization, parvovirus B19, and TTTS. Has largely replaced amniocentesis.

stem: Portal hypertension signs on ultrasound include:
options: Portal vein dilation >13 mm, splenomegaly, ascites, collateral vessels, and hepatofugal portal flow
rationale: Portal HTN: dilated portal vein (>13 mm), splenomegaly, ascites, portosystemic collaterals (recanalized umbilical vein, gastric varices), reversed portal flow.

stem: The spectral Doppler waveform of the common femoral vein normally shows:
options: Spontaneous phasic flow that augments with distal compression and ceases with Valsalva
rationale: Normal CFV: spontaneous phasic flow varying with respiration, augments with calf compression, and transiently ceases with Valsalva. Loss of these features suggests DVT or obstruction.

stem: Complete ICA occlusion vs. near-occlusion on ultrasound:
options: Requires careful assessment — complete occlusion shows no internal flow; near-occlusion shows a tiny thread of flow (string sign)
rationale: Distinguishing total occlusion (no flow) from near-occlusion (tiny residual flow) is critical as near-occlusion may benefit from endarterectomy. Power Doppler increases sensitivity.

stem: Endometrial cancer risk factors that prompt ultrasound evaluation include:
options: Postmenopausal bleeding, endometrial thickness >4-5 mm on TVUS, and tamoxifen use
rationale: PMB warrants TVUS: endometrium >4-5 mm needs tissue sampling. Risk factors: obesity, diabetes, unopposed estrogen, tamoxifen, PCOS, late menopause.

stem: A subchorionic hemorrhage on ultrasound appears as:
options: A crescent-shaped hypoechoic or anechoic collection between the chorion and uterine wall
rationale: Subchorionic hemorrhage: crescent-shaped collection between the gestational sac and uterine wall. Size and location affect prognosis. Most resolve; large ones may increase miscarriage risk.

stem: Lymphocele post-transplant on ultrasound appears as:
options: An anechoic or septated fluid collection adjacent to the transplanted organ
Normal postoperative finding requiring no monitoring
rationale: Lymphocele: peritransplant fluid collection, usually anechoic or with thin septations. Common after renal transplant. May compress the ureter causing hydronephrosis.

stem: The 'keyboard sign' on fetal ultrasound refers to:
options: Echogenic ribs arranged like keyboard keys — seen in normal thoracic imaging and skeletal dysplasia assessment
rationale: The keyboard sign describes the orderly arrangement of fetal ribs on longitudinal thoracic views. Abnormalities in rib length, number, or mineralization suggest skeletal dysplasia.

stem: Renal transplant rejection on ultrasound may show:
options: Increased cortical thickness, elevated RI >0.80, loss of corticomedullary differentiation, and pelvicalyceal dilation
rationale: Acute rejection: increased cortical thickness, elevated RI, loss of CMD, possible pelvicalyceal dilation. However, US findings overlap with other causes; biopsy is definitive.

stem: A phyllodes tumor of the breast on ultrasound appears as:
options: A well-defined, oval, solid mass similar to a large fibroadenoma, possibly with cystic clefts
rationale: Phyllodes tumors: well-defined, solid masses that may be indistinguishable from large fibroadenomas. Cystic clefts/spaces and rapid growth are suggestive. Tissue sampling required.

stem: Peyronie's disease on ultrasound shows:
options: Echogenic plaque on the tunica albuginea of the penis, which may calcify
rationale: Peyronie's: focal fibrotic plaque of the tunica albuginea. US shows hyperechoic focus on the surface of the corpus cavernosum, ± calcification and shadowing.

stem: Bucket handle meniscal tear on ultrasound:
options: Displaced meniscal fragment into the intercondylar notch — difficult to detect by US alone
rationale: Bucket handle tears: central meniscal fragment displaces into the intercondylar notch. US has limited sensitivity for meniscal tears; MRI is the gold standard.

stem: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) on ultrasound may appear as:
options: A solid, hyperechoic or isoechoic cortical mass with possible cystic areas and internal vascularity
rationale: RCC: solid renal mass, variable echogenicity (small RCCs may be hyperechoic mimicking angiomyolipoma). Internal vascularity, heterogeneity, and contour deformity. CT/MRI for staging.

stem: Choledochal cyst on ultrasound shows:
options: Fusiform or saccular dilation of the extrahepatic bile duct communicating with the biliary system
rationale: Choledochal cysts: congenital dilation of the bile ducts. Todani classification: Type I (most common) = fusiform CBD dilation. Associated with increased biliary malignancy risk.

stem: What is the half-value layer (HVL)?
options: The thickness of tissue that reduces intensity by 50%
rationale: HVL = tissue thickness that reduces intensity to half. Related to attenuation coefficient. Higher frequency = shorter HVL = less penetration.

stem: Amplitude mode (A-mode) displays:
options: Echo amplitude as vertical spikes along a horizontal timeline
rationale: A-mode: the original US display showing echo amplitude as vertical deflections vs. depth on a horizontal line. Rarely used now except in ophthalmology.

stem: Horseshoe kidney on ultrasound is characterized by:
options: Connected lower poles anterior to the aorta at the level of the inferior mesenteric artery
A single kidney in the pelvis
rationale: Horseshoe kidney: fused lower poles connected by an isthmus anterior to the aorta. Cannot ascend past the IMA. Associated with Turner syndrome and UPJ obstruction.

stem: Chronic cholecystitis on ultrasound may show:
options: Contracted, thick-walled gallbladder with stones and loss of normal wall layers
rationale: Chronic cholecystitis: small/contracted thick-walled GB, stones, possible wall calcification. May have WES sign. Gallbladder function is impaired.

stem: Meckel's diverticulum in pediatric ultrasound:
options: Appears as a fluid-filled tubular structure in the right lower quadrant arising from the ileum
rationale: Meckel's: a true diverticulum of the ileum, most common congenital GI anomaly. US may show blind-ending fluid-filled loop. Tc-99m pertechnetate scan is more sensitive.

stem: Fetal sacrococcygeal teratoma on ultrasound shows:
options: A solid, cystic, or mixed mass arising from the coccyx/sacral area
rationale: Sacrococcygeal teratoma: the most common neonatal tumor. Mixed solid/cystic mass protruding from the sacrococcygeal area. Large tumors may cause high-output cardiac failure.

stem: The quadruple screen includes:
options: AFP, hCG, estriol, and inhibin A
rationale: Quadruple screen: AFP, hCG, estriol, and inhibin A. Used for trisomy 21 and neural tube defect screening at 15-20 weeks.

stem: Pelvic kidney on ultrasound:
options: The kidney is located in the pelvis and may be mistaken for an adnexal mass
rationale: Pelvic kidney: failure of normal embryologic renal ascent. Found in the pelvis, usually functional. Important to identify to avoid confusion with pelvic masses.

stem: Superficial femoral artery (SFA) stenosis on ultrasound shows:
options: Elevated PSV with PSV ratio >2.0 at the stenosis compared to proximal segment
rationale: SFA stenosis: PSV increase at the stenosis with post-stenotic turbulence. PSV ratio (stenotic/pre-stenotic) >2.0 suggests >50% stenosis. >4.0 suggests >75%.

stem: The saphenofemoral junction assessment in venous insufficiency evaluates:
options: Competence of the terminal valve — reflux >0.5 seconds during Valsalva indicates incompetence
rationale: SFJ assessment: Valsalva or distal compression release tests valve competence. Reflux >0.5 sec = incompetence. Important for varicose vein treatment planning.

stem: Leiomyosarcoma of the uterus on ultrasound:
options: Cannot be reliably distinguished from benign leiomyoma by imaging alone
rationale: Leiomyosarcoma and leiomyoma have overlapping US appearances. Rapid growth, irregular margins, and internal necrosis may raise concern, but biopsy is needed for diagnosis.

stem: A breast intracystic papilloma on ultrasound shows:
options: A solid mural nodule projecting into an otherwise cystic lesion
rationale: Intracystic papilloma: solid mural nodule within a cyst. Color Doppler may show a feeding vessel. Biopsy recommended to rule out intracystic carcinoma.

stem: Thyroid lymphoma on ultrasound typically shows:
options: A markedly hypoechoic, pseudocystic mass with enhanced through-transmission, often in a Hashimoto's background
rationale: Thyroid lymphoma: markedly hypoechoic (pseudocystic) mass, usually in patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Enhanced through-transmission due to homogeneous cellular composition.

stem: A foreign body on ultrasound appears as:
options: A hyperechoic linear or irregular structure with possible reverberation artifact or posterior shadowing
rationale: Foreign bodies (wood, glass, metal) appear hyperechoic with variable artifacts. Wood shows dirty shadow. Glass/metal may show clean shadow and reverberation. US sensitivity ~87-98%.

stem: Olecranon bursitis on ultrasound shows:
options: Fluid collection within the olecranon bursa posterior to the elbow — anechoic if simple, echogenic if infected/hemorrhagic
rationale: Olecranon bursitis: distended bursa posterior to olecranon. Simple (anechoic) = overuse/trauma. Complex (echogenic) = infection/gout/hemorrhage. May show wall thickening.

stem: Acute cholangitis on ultrasound shows:
options: Dilated bile ducts with possible intraluminal debris or pus, CBD stones, and hepatic abscess
rationale: Cholangitis (Charcot's triad: fever, jaundice, RUQ pain): dilated ducts, possible CBD stone, debris/pus in ducts, hepatic abscess formation. US guides drainage.

stem: A thyroglossal duct cyst on ultrasound appears as:
options: A midline cystic structure anterior to the thyroid cartilage/hyoid, which may move with swallowing
rationale: Thyroglossal duct cyst: midline or slightly off-midline, cystic, at/near the hyoid bone. Moves with swallowing and tongue protrusion. Most common pediatric midline neck mass.

stem: Abdominal wall hernia on ultrasound shows:
options: Contents (fat, bowel) protruding through a fascial defect, with real-time assessment showing reducibility
A solid mass within the wall
rationale: Hernia US: identify the fascial defect, contents (fat, bowel), and assess reducibility with Valsalva. Types: umbilical, incisional, inguinal, ventral.

stem: Hepatic candidiasis (microabscesses) on ultrasound shows:
options: Multiple small hypoechoic lesions scattered throughout the liver, often with a 'wheels-within-wheels' or 'target' pattern
rationale: Hepatic candidiasis: multiple small (<2 cm) lesions in immunocompromised patients. Patterns: wheel-within-wheel, target, or uniformly hypoechoic. Often concurrent splenic involvement.

stem: Fetal anencephaly on ultrasound is characterized by:
options: Absence of the cranial vault above the orbits with exposed brain tissue (exencephaly in early gestation)
rationale: Anencephaly: absence of cranial vault and brain tissue above the orbits. The most severe neural tube defect. Incompatible with life. Detectable in first trimester.

stem: Color Doppler 'flash artifact' is caused by:
options: Patient or organ motion creating a burst of color across the image
rationale: Flash artifact: a burst of color from tissue motion (breathing, cardiac motion) rather than blood flow. It fills the entire image briefly and disappears. Not pathological.

stem: Sonographic features of a mature placenta (Grade 3) include:
options: Echogenic indentations extending to the basal layer creating cotyledons, with central fallout areas
rationale: Grade 3 placenta: echogenic indentations reaching the basal plate, creating distinct cotyledons. Central echogenic areas with sonolucent (fallout) zones. Typically seen >35 weeks.

stem: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) on ultrasound shows:
options: Bilaterally enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts of varying sizes replacing normal parenchyma
rationale: ADPKD: bilateral renal enlargement with multiple cysts of various sizes. Liver and pancreatic cysts may coexist. Screening criteria: ≥3 cysts total in patients aged 15-39 with family history.

stem: Ureteral jet on ultrasound:
options: Appears as a pulsing color Doppler signal at the ureteral orifice in the bladder, indicating ureteral patency
rationale: Ureteral jets: periodic bursts of flow from the ureteral orifices into the bladder, visible on color Doppler. Absence of a jet suggests ipsilateral ureteral obstruction.

stem: Post-void residual (PVR) bladder volume is measured to assess:
options: Bladder emptying adequacy — PVR >100-200 mL may indicate urinary retention or outlet obstruction
rationale: PVR measures residual urine after voiding. Volume = L × W × H × 0.523. PVR >200 mL suggests significant urinary retention requiring evaluation.

stem: Nutcracker syndrome causes:
options: Left renal vein hypertension leading to hematuria, left flank pain, and possibly left-sided varicocele
rationale: Nutcracker: LRV compression between SMA and aorta → left renal venous hypertension. Symptoms: hematuria, left flank pain, proteinuria, and left gonadal vein dilation (varicocele).

stem: A transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of the bladder on ultrasound may show:
options: An echogenic mass projecting into the bladder lumen from the wall, with possible internal vascularity
rationale: TCC: echogenic, papillary mass protruding into the bladder lumen. May show internal vascularity on color Doppler. Usually arises from the posterior/lateral wall near ureteral orifices.

stem: A pediatric pyloric stenosis on ultrasound shows:
options: Thickened pyloric muscle (>3 mm) and elongated pyloric channel (>15 mm) with failure of gastric contents to pass
rationale: Pyloric stenosis: muscle thickness >3 mm, channel length >15 mm, pyloric diameter >15 mm. The 'cervix sign' and real-time failure of passage confirm diagnosis.

stem: Liver transplant Doppler evaluation focuses on:
options: Hepatic artery patency, portal vein flow, hepatic vein outflow, and IVC patency
rationale: Post-transplant US: hepatic artery PSV/RI (RI <0.5 or >0.8 is concerning), portal vein flow direction/velocity, hepatic vein waveform, IVC, and biliary evaluation.
